title: Squab pie
subtitle: The West Country lamb, apple, and onion pie
excerpt: Despite the name, squab pie contains no pigeon. The West Country dish is a deep pie of diced lamb, sliced apple, and onion in a cider gravy, topped with shortcrust pastry. Regional, old, and well worth knowing.
sourceNotes: Squab pie is documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, as a West Country and Cornish regional dish. Despite the name (squab referring to young pigeon in standard usage), the West Country version is made with lamb or mutton rather than bird. The lamb, apple, and onion combination with shortcrust pastry lid is attested as the standard regional form in Devon and Cornwall.

content.text: The name is misleading. In most of England, squab means young pigeon, but in Devon and Cornwall the word has long been applied to a deep lamb and apple pie with no bird in it. The combination of lamb, sharp apple, and onion is well established in West Country cooking, the sweetness of the apple cutting the richness of the meat. The pie is topped with a
lid only, with no pastry base, the filling cooks in its own juices and the base of the dish catches the gravy.
The filling is cooked on the hob before the pastry goes on, so the pie only needs enough oven time to cook the pastry through. This keeps the filling tender and the gravy well reduced before baking.

attrs.prepNote: cold, for the pastry; or use 175 g all-butter
cold, for the pastry; plus 1 tbsp for browning the meat
sharp or cooking apple, peeled, cored, and sliced

content.text: Rub {{lard}} and the pastry-quantity cold {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add 5 to 6 tablespoons of cold water and bring together to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Season {{lamb-shoulder}} with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Melt the browning-quantity {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over high heat. Brown the lamb in two batches until well coloured on all sides, then set aside. In the same pan, soften {{onion}} over medium heat for 8 to 10 minutes. Return the lamb to the pan with {{apple}}, {{dry-cider}}, and {{thyme-fresh}}. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 45 minutes until the lamb is tender and the gravy has reduced to a thick, glossy consistency. Discard the thyme sprigs. Transfer the filling to a deep oval or round pie dish about 24 cm across. Leave to cool for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Roll the pastry to 3 to 4 mm thick and cut to fit the pie dish with a 2 cm overhang. Drape the pastry over the filled dish, press the edges to seal, and trim. Brush with beaten {{eggs}}. Cut two small steam vents in the centre. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and firm to the touch. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.
Mutton gives a deeper flavour than lamb and is the traditional choice in older recipes. The cooking time should be increased to 60 to 70 minutes to ensure tenderness.
A sharp cooking apple such as Bramley breaks down and thickens the gravy slightly. An eating apple holds its shape better and gives a cleaner texture.
The filling must be fully cooked and the gravy well reduced before the pastry goes on; an under-reduced filling will make the pastry soggy.
